title: Why Kundalini Awakening Can Look Exactly Like a Manic Episode

body:
Why Kundalini Awakening Can Look Exactly Like a Manic Episode

You're probably here because you're scared, right? You're sitting there, maybe it's 3 AM, and your mind is racing. Your body feels like it's plugged into a wall socket. You're seeing things, hearing things, feeling things that don't make any damn sense. Your friends and family are looking at you sideways, or they've already backed away. Someone's probably even muttered "bipolar" or "mania." And you're wondering if you're losing your mind, or if there's something else going on. I've been there. I know that feeling of absolute terror and isolation. That's why I'm talking to you like this, straight up, no bullshit. Because you need to hear it from someone who's walked through that fire and come out the other side.

Look, the truth is, what you're experiencing - all that intense energy, the sleeplessness, the rapid thoughts, the feeling of being connected to something massive and overwhelming - it can absolutely, 100% mimic a manic episode. It's not a coincidence. The symptoms overlap so much it's dizzying. Doctors, therapists, even your well-meaning loved ones, they're trained to spot mania. They see the energy, the grandiosity, the lack of sleep, and they'll jump to the most obvious conclusion based on their training. And sometimes, yeah, it is mania. But sometimes, it's something else entirely. Something far stranger, far more ancient, and in its own way, far more terrifying if you don't know what it is.

I'm talking about a kundalini awakening, or what's sometimes called a spiritual emergency. It's not in the DSM-5. Your doctor probably hasn't heard of it, or if they have, they've dismissed it as woo-woo. But here's the thing: people have been talking about this for thousands of years, across cultures, in different languages. It's real, and it can hit you like a freight train, leaving you feeling like your entire reality has shattered. You're not alone. You're not crazy, not necessarily. You're just in a place very few people understand.

The Overlapping Symptoms: Why It's So Confusing

Let's get down to brass tacks. Why do these two things - a manic episode and a kundalini awakening - look so damn similar? Because they both involve an extreme upregulation of your entire system. It's like your internal dimmer switch got cranked to 11, and now you're buzzing with energy you don't know what to do with.

Energy and Sleep

You probably haven't slept much, have you? Maybe a few hours here and there, or none at all for days. And yet, you don't feel tired. You feel wired, charged, like you could run a marathon or write a novel in one sitting. That's classic mania. It's also classic kundalini. The energy coursing through you is so intense, it often disrupts normal sleep patterns. Your body is trying to process something massive, and it doesn't want to shut down. I remember weeks where I'd get maybe two hours of sleep a night, and I was still functioning, albeit barely. My mind was racing, too fast to catch. I'd try to meditate, and it felt like I was trying to hold a wild animal still. You know that feeling, right?

The difference, if there is one, often comes down to the quality of the sleeplessness. In traditional mania, it's often accompanied by a feeling of restless agitation, an inability to settle. With kundalini, while there's certainly restlessness, there can also be periods of intense inner peace or visionary states during these sleepless hours. It's not just a lack of sleep; it's an experience during the lack of sleep. But honestly, in the thick of it, you might not be able to tell the difference yourself. It all just feels like too much.

Thought Patterns and Grandiosity

Your thoughts are probably racing. You're making connections nobody else seems to see. You might feel like you've got access to some secret knowledge, some cosmic truth. You might feel like you're special, chosen, or that you have a mission to accomplish. Maybe you feel a sudden surge of creativity, a desire to write, paint, compose music, or start a new business. This is where the "grandiosity" often comes in - a feeling of inflated self-esteem, an exaggerated sense of one's own importance or abilities. This is a hallmark of mania.

But guess what? It's also a common symptom of kundalini awakening. When this energy starts moving, it can blow open your perception of reality. You might genuinely feel like you're receiving divine messages, that you're connected to all of existence, that you're here to change the world. You might actually experience moments of deep insight and clarity that feel like superpowers. The challenge is discerning whether these insights are truly coming from a deeper place of wisdom, or if they're simply a product of an overactive, dysregulated brain. And when you're in the middle of it, you can't trust your own judgment. That's why you need support, people who can ground you.

I remember thinking I could solve all the world's problems, that I was a prophet. I had notebooks full of scribbled ideas, visions, plans. My logical mind was screaming, "This is insane!" But another part of me felt absolutely convinced it was real. And that's terrifying because you don't know which voice to trust. You know what I mean?

Sensory Overload and Psychosis

Are you seeing colors more vibrantly? Hearing sounds more acutely? Feeling energy moving through your body, tingling, buzzing, vibrating? Maybe you're even having visions, hearing voices, or experiencing telepathy. These are often labeled as "psychotic features" in a manic episode - hallucinations, delusions, disorganized thought. And yes, they can be.

But with kundalini, these aren't necessarily signs of psychosis. They can be genuine perceptual changes, a widening of your sensory bandwidth. The energy can manifest as heat, cold, vibrations, spontaneous movements (kriyas). You might genuinely perceive energetic fields, auras, or experience what feels like direct communication with non-physical entities. You might have visions that feel incredibly real, or hear internal sounds that aren't auditory hallucinations but distinct inner experiences. The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link) goes into a lot of these physical and psychological manifestations, and reading it might make you feel a little less alone in what you're going through. It's not just you.

The key here is whether these experiences are causing significant distress, impairing your functioning, or leading to self-destructive behavior. If you're hearing voices telling you to harm yourself or others, that's a medical emergency, no matter what you think the cause is. But if you're hearing what feels like guidance, or seeing visions that feel meaningful, but you're still able to hold down a job (barely), feed yourself, and maintain some semblance of reality, then the waters get very muddy. This is where the distinction is vital, and also incredibly hard to make, especially for someone who hasn't experienced it.

The Key Differentiators: What's the Underlying Current?

So, if the symptoms are so similar, how do you tell the difference? It's not easy, and honestly, sometimes it's both. You can absolutely have a spiritual emergency that triggers or is intertwined with a manic episode. The human psyche isn't neat and tidy. But here are some things to consider, things I wish someone had told me when I was going through it:

  • The Narrative and Meaning: With traditional mania, while there can be grandiosity, the narrative often feels disjointed, chaotic, and lacking a coherent spiritual or existential meaning. With kundalini, even amidst the chaos, there's often a feeling of deep purpose, a sense that something deep is happening, even if it's terrifying. You might feel like you're being purified, initiated, or transformed. There's an underlying spiritual approach, however messy it feels.
  • The Physical Sensations: Kundalini is often described as a physical energy moving through the body - heat, vibrations, pressure, spontaneous movements (kriyas), or even specific sensations in the spine or chakras. While mania can cause restlessness, it typically doesn't involve these distinct, often powerful, internal physical phenomena. The Kundalini Guide by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) describes many of these physical symptoms in detail, which can be validating if you're experiencing them.
  • The Aftermath: After a manic episode, people often crash into depression, feel depleted, and regret many of their actions. While a kundalini crisis can certainly involve periods of depression and exhaustion, there's also often a sense of long-term growth, expanded awareness, and a deeper connection to self and spirit, even if the process was incredibly painful. It's a difficult birth, but a birth nonetheless.
  • Triggers: Mania can be triggered by stress, sleep deprivation, certain medications, or simply appear spontaneously. Kundalini awakenings are often triggered by spiritual practices (meditation, yoga, breathwork), intense emotional experiences, trauma, or even spontaneous events that seem to have no logical explanation.
  • The Presence of "Kriyas": These are involuntary physical movements - shaking, trembling, spontaneous yoga poses, vocalizations. They're not typically seen in pure manic episodes, but are a common feature of kundalini. They can be incredibly disturbing and feel like you've lost control of your body.

What To Do When You're In The Thick Of It

First, breathe. Just breathe. You're still here. You're reading this. That's a good sign. It means you're still trying to make sense of things, which is more than some people can do when they're truly lost.

Here's what I'll tell you from my own experience, and from seeing countless others go through it:

  1. Get Professional Help, But Be Smart About It: Go see a doctor. Tell them everything you're experiencing. Don't hold back. They might diagnose you with bipolar disorder. They might prescribe medication. And honestly, sometimes medication is necessary to stabilize your system, to bring you back down to earth so you can even begin to process what's happening. It's not a failure to take medication if you need it. It can give you the breathing room to figure things out. But also, look for therapists or counselors who are open to spiritual emergencies, who understand transpersonal psychology. They're rare, but they exist. When Spirit Leaps: Navigating the Process of Spiritual Awakening by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) is a great resource if you want to understand the process better and find guidance.
  2. Ground Yourself: This is CRITICAL. When you're flying high, you need to come back down.
    • Nature: Get outside. Walk barefoot on the grass. Touch trees. Sit by water. Connect with the earth.
    • Food: Eat nourishing, grounding foods. Root vegetables, soups, stews. Avoid stimulants like caffeine and sugar.
    • Routine: Try to maintain some semblance of a daily routine, even if it feels impossible. Regular meal times, a consistent (even if short) sleep schedule.
    • Physical Activity: Gentle exercise like walking, stretching, or slow yoga can help move the energy without overstimulating you.
    • Sensory Input: Listen to calming music, take warm baths, use essential oils. Anything that brings your senses back to the physical world in a gentle way.
  3. Limit Stimulation: Turn off social media. Limit news. Avoid intense conversations or arguments. Your nervous system is already overloaded. Don't add to it.
  4. Find Support: This is hard, because many people won't understand. But try to find at least one person you trust - a friend, a family member, a spiritual teacher - who you can talk to openly, without judgment. If you can't find someone in person, look for online communities or forums dedicated to spiritual awakening or kundalini. Just be discerning, as there's a lot of misinformation out there.
  5. Educate Yourself (Carefully): Reading about kundalini can be both terrifying and validating. It can help you put a name to what you're experiencing. But don't obsess over every symptom. Just get a general understanding. Sean Blackwell's Am I Bipolar or Waking Up? (paid link) is exactly the kind of book you need right now. It directly addresses the confusion you're feeling.
  6. Surrender, But Don't Be Reckless: This energy is powerful. Fighting it constantly will only exhaust you. There's a certain amount of surrender that's necessary, allowing the process to unfold. But surrender doesn't mean being reckless. Don't quit your job, blow all your money, or make huge life changes while you're in an acute state. Grounding and self-care are still your top priorities.

The Long Road Back (or Forward)

This isn't a quick fix. This is a long, arduous journey. You might feel like you're being broken down to be rebuilt. And in a sense, you are. Your old self, your old ways of seeing the world, they're probably not going to survive this intact. It's painful, it's disorienting, and it's incredibly lonely.

But here's the hope: if it is a spiritual awakening, there's a purpose to this chaos. There's a deeper intelligence at play, even if it feels completely out of control right now. You're being pushed to grow, to shed old skins, to become more of who you truly are. It's not a comfortable process. It's often brutal. But many, many people come through this stronger, wiser, and with a deep sense of connection to life that they never had before.

So yeah, it looks like mania. It feels like mania. It might even be treated like mania, and that might be exactly what you need to get stable. But don't lose sight of the possibility that there's something more happening here. Hold onto that thread of meaning, even if it's just a tiny whisper. You're not alone in this dark night. Keep breathing. Keep seeking. You'll find your way.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
